title: Latest News
text:
CALM LAB


Control of Attention in Life and Mind


University of North Carolina Wilmington




The CALM Lab studies attention control across contexts of life and mind, combining cognitive science, neuroscience, and contemplative approaches to understand how attention shapes thought, success, and well-being.








Team
Meet the researchers and students working in the CALM Lab.
Meet the Team →








Projects
Explore our research on attention control, brain networks, and mindfulness.
Explore Projects →








Connect
Interested in collaborating, joining the lab, or learning more about our work?
Get in Touch →

title: CALM LAB
CALM LAB
CALM LAB
CALM LAB
CALM LAB
CALM LAB
Jason S. Tsukahara, Ph.D.
text:
Jason S. Tsukahara, Ph.D.

Principal Investigator


Dr. Tsukahara directs the CALM Lab and studies attention control across contexts of life and mind. His research integrates cognitive psychology, neuroscience, and contemplative science to understand the nature and potentials of attention.








Department
UNC Wilmington, Department of Psychology


Contact
jason.tsukahara@miami.edu 


Office
Teachers Lab Room 123








Research Interests
This is a longer paragraph style explanation of my research interests into something I don’t know how to do this but here we go, yipee kaya. Then this is a part of it as well. And what if it keeps on going on for a longer and longer and longer ezplanation. But does not stop here yet.

About
Jane Doe is a graduate student in the CALM Lab studying how attention fluctuates over time and how contemplative practices may help support cognitive performance and well-being. Her research focuses on understanding the dynamics of sustained attention—why focus is sometimes stable and at other times drifts—and how these fluctuations shape thought, behavior, and performance. By combining behavioral experiments with neurocognitive approaches, she investigates how attention operates both in everyday contexts and in situations that demand sustained mental effort.
In her research, Jane is particularly interested in the relationship between mind wandering and cognitive control. She studies how internal thoughts emerge during ongoing tasks and how individuals differ in their ability to maintain goal-directed focus. Her work draws on behavioral tasks and physiological measures such as EEG to examine the moment-to-moment dynamics of attention and the neural systems that support these processes.
More broadly, Jane is interested in how contemplative practices such as mindfulness may influence attention and emotional well-being. She is motivated by the possibility that training attention can enhance both cognitive performance and quality of life. Outside the lab, Jane enjoys exploring nature, reading, and practicing mindfulness meditation—interests that often inspire new questions about the mind and its capacity for focus and reflection.





 Back to top

title: CALM Lab website launches
text:
We are excited to announce the launch of the CALM Lab website! This website serves as a hub for our research, publications, projects, and news. We hope it will be a valuable resource for students, researchers, and anyone interested in the science of attention control. Please explore the site and feel free to reach out if you have any questions.

title: Individual Differences and Cognitive Mechanisms
text:
PROJECTS


Research areas and ongoing lines of work in the CALM Lab.




The CALM Lab’s research is organized around three interconnected areas that together aim to understand attention across contexts of life and mind.





Cognitive Mechanisms
How do differences in the ability to control attention relate to cognition and well-being?
View Project →





Neural Mechanisms
What are the neural mechanisms underlying the ability to control attention?
View Project →





Mindfulness
How can mindfulness and contemplative practices strengthen attention control and well-being?
View Project →





Individual Differences and Cognitive Mechanisms

How do differences in the ability to control attention relate to cognition and well-being?


People differ in their ability to control attention, and these differences are a fundamental aspect of human cognition. We investigate how individual differences in attention control play a role in shaping our perception, memory, reasoning, and well-being.
Critical to understanding the role of attention control in cognition and well-being is the ability to measure it. We investigate how to best measure attention control, including the development of new tasks and approaches to measuring attention control in real-world contexts.
View and Download Attention Tasks →

Neural Mechanisms

What are the neural mechanisms underlying the ability to control attention?


We investigate the neural mechanisms underlying attention control, including the role of large-scale brain networks and their interactions in supporting attention control across contexts of life and mind.

Mindfulness and Contemplative Practices

How can mindfulness and contemplative practices strengthen attention control and well-being?


Mindfulness and related contemplative practices have been shown to influence attention, cognitive performance, and resilience. We investigate how these practices can strengthen attention control and promote well-being.
